items.title: Migrate quadlet healthchecks from curl to Python urllib (fixes #61)
items.body:
Closes #61

## Problem
9 quadlet files use `curl -sf` in HealthCmd, but UBI10-minimal does not include curl. This causes healthchecks to fail silently on UBI10-minimal base images.

## Solution
Replace all curl-based HealthCmd with Python urllib.request:
```ini
HealthCmd=python3 -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:<port>/health')"
```

9 files changed, matching the pattern used by ragorchestrator.container and open-webui.container (which already use Python healthchecks).

## Testing
- 87 shell tests passing (including `check_no_curl_in_healthcmd` validator)
- `grep -r "HealthCmd=curl" quadlets/ hosts/` returns no matches

## Verification
```bash
grep -r "curl" quadlets/ hosts/ | grep -i healthcmd
# Expected: no output
```

items.title: Taint engine: port to Go with go/taint-* rules (refs #31)
items.body:
## Summary

Adds a third intraprocedural taint engine (`src/rules/go_taint.rs`) mirroring the Python and JavaScript surfaces, plus three first-consumer Go taint rules wired into `src/rules/go.rs`:

- `go/taint-command-injection` (CWE-78, Critical)
- `go/taint-sql-injection`      (CWE-89, Critical) — uses `NodeMatcher::MethodName` to cover `db.Query` / `tx.Exec` / `stmt.QueryRow` / `gorm.DB.Raw` without enumerating receiver names
- `go/taint-ssrf`               (CWE-918, High)

Engine features ported in full from Python/JS:

- flow-insensitive per-function / per-method analysis
- one-level selector (`x.y`) and index (`m[k]`) propagation
- wrapping-call propagation (`string(tainted)`, `[]byte(tainted)`, `fmt.Sprintf("%s", tainted)`)
- method-call propagation on tainted receivers (`r.URL.Query().Get("x")`)
- binary `+` string-concat propagation (mirrors the JS engine)
- sanitizer collapse-to-clean
- `GoImportAliases` for `import f "fmt"` grouped / aliased specs (dot and side-effect imports documented out of scope)
- two-pass same-file interprocedural return summaries
- native Go multi-return destructuring (`a, b := f()`) via a shared LHS/RHS pairing helper used by `short_var_declaration`, `var_spec`, and `assignment_statement`

Shared sources in `go_taint_sources()` cover net/http (`r`/`req`/`request` ParamName + `r.URL|Header|Body|Form` attributes + `r.FormValue`/`r.PostFormValue`/`r.URL.Query` calls), Gin `*Context`, Echo, Fiber, and generic `os.Getenv`/`os.Args`. `c` is intentionally NOT a `ParamName` source to avoid colliding with generic single-letter locals.

`FileContext` gains a `go_aliases` slot and `scanner.rs` builds `GoImportAliases` for `.go` files only, mirroring the Python/JS wiring.

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build --release`
- [x] `cargo test` — 212 tests, all green (18 new go_taint unit tests + 3 new integration tests + 1 new realistic_fixtures test)
- [x] `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- [x] `cargo fmt`
- [x] `cargo run --bin gen_rules_ts > /tmp/regen.ts && diff -u www/src/data/rules.ts /tmp/regen.ts` — empty diff (rules.ts regenerated and committed)
- [x] Manual: `./target/release/foxguard tests/fixtures/realistic/gin_app.go` — 7 findings (3 taint + 3 conservative injection + 1 gin-no-trusted-proxies)
- [x] Manual: `./target/release/foxguard tests/fixtures/safe_go_taint.go` — 0 `go/taint-*` findings
- [x] `docs/taint-tracking.md` updated with "Supported Go frameworks" + Go-specific engine notes + out-of-scope list
- [x] `docs/precision.md` Tier 4 section lists the three new rules

none

items.title: fix: YAML parse errors in deploy and CAB bridge workflows
items.body:
Two workflows have been completely broken since creation — every single run (138+ deploys) fails with 0 jobs because GitHub cannot parse the YAML.

### Root cause

YAML block scalars (`run: |`) terminate when a line has less indentation than the first content line. Both workflows contain content at column 1 inside indented block scalars, which the YAML parser interprets as the end of the scalar and then fails on.

### `deploy-to-zhuyuan-server.yml`

Nested heredoc inside SSH heredoc — `ENV_EOF` body at column 1 breaks the outer `run: |` block:

```yaml
# Before (broken): heredoc content at column 1
          cat > /opt/age-os/.env.mcp << 'ENV_EOF'
NODE_ENV=production        # ← column 1, YAML parser chokes
MCP_PORT=3100
ENV_EOF

# After: echo commands, properly indented
          echo "NODE_ENV=production" > /opt/age-os/.env.mcp
          echo "MCP_PORT=3100" >> /opt/age-os/.env.mcp
          echo "MCP_BIND_HOST=127.0.0.1" >> /opt/age-os/.env.mcp
```

### `copilot-dev-bridge.yml`

Multi-line inline Python at column 1 inside a `run: |` block:

```yaml
# Before (broken): Python body at column 1
            TITLE=$(echo "$ISSUE_JSON" | python3 -c "
import sys, json              # ← column 1
try:
    data = json.load(sys.stdin)
...")

# After: single-line Python
            TITLE=$(echo "$ISSUE_JSON" | python3 -c "import sys, json; ...")
```

`chmod 600` on `.env.mcp` is already present at line 304, unchanged.

items.title: Port Prime Utilities To Rust
items.body:
## What

work on phase 7 issue #786.

Closes #786

## Artifacts

| File | Path |
|------|------|
| Plan | `.flow-states/port-prime-utilities-to-rust-plan.md` |
| DAG | `.flow-states/port-prime-utilities-to-rust-dag.md` |
| Log | `.flow-states/port-prime-utilities-to-rust.log` |
| State | `.flow-states/port-prime-utilities-to-rust.json` |
| Transcript | `/Users/ben/.claude/projects/-Users-ben-code-flow/dd2432f3-443d-41a1-9ea5-c7f3f9b28047.jsonl` |

## Plan

<details>
<summary>Implementation plan</summary>

````text
# Implementation Plan — Port prime utilities to Rust (#786)

## Context

Phase 7 of the Python-to-Rust port. Issue #786 requires moving four prime-adjacent utilities from `lib/*.py` to the `flow-rs` binary as first-class clap subcommands:

- `lib/prime-check.py` (132 lines) → `flow prime-check`
- `lib/prime-project.py` (107 lines) → `flow prime-project`
- `lib/detect-framework.py` (111 lines) → `flow detect-framework`
- `lib/promote-permissions.py` (93 lines) → `flow promote-permissions`

Acceptance criteria: Rust tests covering `test_prime_check.py` (264 lines), `test_prime_project.py` (124 lines), `test_promote_permissions.py` (315 lines); delete corresponding Python files; `bin/ci` passes. `lib/prime-setup.py` is out of scope as a port target but has a runtime dependency on `prime-project.py` that must be broken to enable deletion. `test_detect_framework.py` (183 lines) is omitted from the AC test-port list but must also be ported — otherwise deleting `lib/detect-framework.py` leaves a dangling test import.

## Exploration

Files read during planning:

- `lib/prime-check.py`, `lib/prime-project.py`, `lib/detect-framework.py`, `lib/promote-permissions.py` — full bodies
- `lib/prime-setup.py` — relevant sections: `compute_config_hash` and `compute_setup_hash` at lines 141-162, `UNIVERSAL_ALLOW`/`FLOW_DENY`/`EXCLUDE_ENTRIES` at lines 36-108, `_import_sibling` at lines 27-33, prime-project import at lines 542-543, create-dependencies subprocess precedent at lines 545-556
- `tests/test_prime_check.py` (15 test cases), `tests/test_prime_project.py` (11 test cases), `tests/test_detect_framework.py` (17 test cases), `tests/test_promote_permissions.py` (18 test cases)
- `tests/test_prime_setup.py` lines 1085-1135 — prime_project integration tests
- `tests/test_log_bridge.py` — Python bridge pattern precedent
- `tests/conftest.py` — shared fixtures (LIB_DIR, FRAMEWORKS_DIR, import_lib)
- `tests/upgrade_check.rs`, `tests/check_phase.rs` — existing Rust integration test patterns (CARGO_BIN_EXE_flow-rs, tempfile::tempdir, Command::output())
- `src/lib.rs`, `src/main.rs` — module registration and clap subcommand dispatch
- `src/utils.rs` — `plugin_root()`, `frameworks_dir()`, `read_version_from()` helpers already available
- `src/commands/init_state.rs` — reference for a prior port's structure
- `frameworks/*/detect.json` — actual glob patterns in use: `Gemfile`, `Cargo.toml`, `go.mod`, `pyproject.toml`, `setup.py`, `requirements.txt`, `*.xcodeproj`
- `bin/flow` — hybrid dispatcher (Rust-first with Python fallback on exit 127, auto-rebuild on stale)
- `bin/ci` — confirmed `cargo build --quiet` runs BEFORE `pytest` (line 22-23 then line 26), so subprocess-dependent pytest tests always see an up-to-date Rust binary
- `Cargo.toml` — existing deps include `serde_json` with `preserve_order`, `indexmap` with `serde`, `sha2`, `clap` derive; `tempfile` and `filetime` in dev-deps

Key runtime dependency facts (verified by grep):

- Only **one** Python file imports any of the four targets: `lib/prime-setup.py` line 542 imports `prime-project` via `_import_sibling`. The other three have **no** Python importers.
- `lib/prime-check.py` has a **logical** dependency on `lib/prime-setup.py` (loads it dynamically to call `compute_config_hash` and `compute_setup_hash`). This is not an import in the usual sense — `prime-check.py` loads `prime-setup.py` at runtime.
- 20 files reference the four scripts; 17 are prose-only (skills, docs, CLAUDE.md, RELEASE-NOTES, test_structural.py, test_skill_contracts.py). None need edits.

Rust infrastructure already available (verified in `src/utils.rs`):

- `plugin_root() -> Option<PathBuf>` — walks up from `current_exe()` to find `flow-phases.json`, honors `CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT` env var override
- `frameworks_dir() -> Option<PathBuf>` — returns `plugin_root().join("frameworks")`
- `read_version_from(path: &Path) -> String` — reads `version` field from a plugin.json
- `sha2::{Digest, Sha256}` already imported

## Risks

1. **Hash byte-parity (correctness-critical).** Python `json.dumps(canonical, sort_keys=True)` emits output with `(", ", ": ")` default separators. Rust `serde_json::to_string` uses `(",", ":")`. The resulting SHA-256 digests differ. Without a fix, every auto-upgrade path for every user fails silently (Rust says "version mismatch" even though hashes logically agree). **Mitigation:** implement a custom `serde_json::ser::Formatter` (`PythonDefaultFormatter`) that emits Python's default separators, and verify with an end-to-end round-trip test in Task 18 (Python computes hash, Rust auto-upgrades using that hash).

2. **Constant duplication drift.** `UNIVERSAL_ALLOW` (48 entries), `FLOW_DENY` (10), `EXCLUDE_ENTRIES` (5) live in `lib/prime-setup.py` and must be duplicated in Rust for the port. Any future edit to Python constants without updating Rust produces a hash mismatch that breaks auto-upgrade. **Mitigation:** static parity test in Task 17 (reads both files, asserts entry-by-entry equality with a detailed diff on failure).

3. **test_prime_setup.py regression after subprocess switch.** After Task 20 edits `prime-setup.py` to subprocess-call `bin/flow prime-project`, the test_prime_setup.py assertions `test_cli_primes_project_claude_md` and `test_cli_prime_project_error_does_not_block_success` depend on the Rust binary being built. Verified: `bin/ci` line 22-23 runs `cargo build --quiet` before `pytest` on line 26. **Low risk but verified.**

4. **Empty-string `flow_version`.** Python `init_data.get("flow_version") != plugin_version` treats `""` as a valid value that fails version equality. Rust `Option<&str>` must filter empty strings explicitly (`.filter(|s| !s.is_empty())`) per rust-port-parity.md Empty-String vs Missing-Key Falsy Equivalence. Omitting this silently changes semantics for corrupted `.flow.json` files.

5. **Auto-upgrade writeback format.** Python rewrites `.flow.json` with `json.dumps(init_data) + "\n"` — no indent, insertion-order preserved. Rust must match: `serde_json::to_string(&data)` (NOT `to_string_pretty`) + `"\n"`, with `preserve_order` feature already enabled (confirmed in Cargo.toml).

6. **compute_setup_hash path resolution.** Python uses `Path(__file__).resolve()` to read `prime-setup.py` bytes. Rust must resolve `plugin_root().join("lib/prime-setup.py")`. Different path strategies, same file.

7. **glob matcher directory semantics.** Python `Path.glob("*.xcodeproj")` matches entries by name suffix. Rust inline matcher must use `entry.file_name().ends_with(".xcodeproj")` — NOT `entry.path().extension()` — because `.xcodeproj` targets are **directories**. `test_detects_ios_when_xcodeproj_exists` creates `project/MyApp.xcodeproj` as a directory, which has no extension by `Path::extension()` semantics.

8. **Marker byte-offset slicing in prime-project.** Python `existing_content[end_index] == "\n"` is a char-index check. Markers (`<!-- FLOW:BEGIN -->`, `<!-- FLOW:END -->`) are ASCII, so Rust byte-index slicing is safe. **Documented invariant required** per rust-port-parity.md String Slicing section.

9. **Write-error test fixtures on macOS vs Linux.** Python tests use monkeypatched `open()`. Rust equivalents use `chmod 0o444` on target files or `0o555` on parent dirs. Both macOS and Linux honor these modes for regular-user temp files. Low risk.

10. **Custom Formatter implementation risk.** `serde_json::ser::Formatter` has 20+ methods; only 3 need overrides (`begin_object_key`, `begin_object_value`, `begin_array_value`). Missing a method leaves default behavior, which is correct for everything except separator spacing. Verified by end-to-end hash parity test — if any separator is wrong, the test fails.

## Approach

**Port pattern (applied to each of the 4 utilities):**

Each Rust module follows the same shape:

- Pure logic function (e.g. `fn prime(...) -> Value`, `fn detect(...) -> Vec<..>`) — unit testable
- `run_impl(args: &Args) -> Result<Value, String>` — CLI layer, reads args, does I/O, returns JSON value or error string
- `run(args)` — thin exit wrapper that prints the JSON and `process::exit(code)` on error
- `#[derive(clap::Args)] struct Args { ... }` for CLI flags

This is the `run_impl` extraction pattern from rust-port-parity.md so CLI error-path tests can target `run_impl` directly (process::exit would terminate the test binary).

**Dependency break for prime-project:**

`lib/prime-setup.py` currently imports `prime-project.py` at line 542. After porting, `prime-setup.py` will subprocess-call `bin/flow prime-project` using the **exact same pattern** already used for `create-dependencies` at lines 545-556. This is a one-region edit to `prime-setup.py` — not a port — and it unblocks full deletion of `prime-project.py`.

**Hash function strategy for prime-check:**

- `compute_setup_hash()` = `sha256(read(plugin_root/lib/prime-setup.py))` hex[:12]. Trivial.
- `compute_config_hash(framework)` duplicates `UNIVERSAL_ALLOW`/`FLOW_DENY`/`EXCLUDE_ENTRIES` as `const &[&str]` arrays in Rust, loads framework `permissions.json`, builds a `BTreeMap<String, Value>` with sorted allow/deny/exclude lists, serializes via a custom `PythonDefaultFormatter` that emits `(", ", ": ")` separators, then SHA-256 hex[:12].

Two parity safeguards (Task 17, Task 18):
- Static: Python test reads `src/prime_check.rs`, extracts the three const arrays via regex, and asserts each entry matches the Python list.
- End-to-end: Python test imports `prime_setup.compute_config_hash("rails")`, writes a `.flow.json` with old version and that hash, invokes `bin/flow prime-check`, asserts `auto_upgraded: true`. Repeats for all 5 frameworks.

**TDD discipline:**

For every Rust module: write the integration tests first (against a non-existent module — the test file compiles against nothing yet), then implement the module. The `tests/<name>.rs` file targets `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_flow-rs")` so tests drive the full binary. Every `Command` call in test modules uses `.output()` (never `.status()`) per rust-port-parity.md Test-Module Subprocess Stdio.

**Commit atomicity:**

Each Rust port (tests + impl + wiring) can land as one or more commits. The prime-setup.py edit (Task 20), Python deletions (Tasks 21-22), and tombstones (Task 23) must land as a single commit to avoid a red intermediate state where `prime-setup.py` still references a deleted Python file. Task 19 (parity test) lands before deletions so the contract is active during deletion.

## Dependency Graph

| Task | Type | Depends On |
|------|------|------------|
| 1. Write `tests/detect_framework.rs` | test | — |
| 2. Implement `src/detect_framework.rs` | implement | 1 |
| 3. Write `tests/promote_permissions.rs` | test | — |
| 4. Implement `src/promote_permissions.rs` | implement | 3 |
| 5. Write `tests/prime_project.rs` | test | — |
| 6. Implement `src/prime_project.rs` | implement | 5 |
| 7. Write `tests/prime_check.rs` (happy path + errors) | test | — |
| 8. Write `tests/prime_check.rs` auto-upgrade cases | test | 7 |
| 9. Implement `src/prime_check.rs` — constants + `PythonDefaultFormatter` | implement | 8 |
| 10. Implement `src/prime_check.rs` — `compute_config_hash`, `compute_setup_hash`, `read_flow_json` helper | implement | 9 |
| 11. Implement `src/prime_check.rs` — `run_impl` + error messages + writeback | implement | 10 |

### Task 4 — Implement `src/promote_permissions.rs`

Create `src/promote_permissions.rs` with:

- `#[derive(clap::Args)] struct Args { #[arg(long = "worktree-path")] worktree_path: String }`
- `fn promote(worktree_path: &Path) -> Value` — pure function:
  - Check `.claude/settings.local.json` exists → `{"status":"skipped","reason":"no_local_file"}` if not
  - Parse local JSON; bundle any `serde_json::Error` or `io::Error` as `{"status":"error","message":"Could not parse settings.local.json: <e>"}`
  - Extract `permissions.allow` via `.get().and_then().and_then(as_array).cloned().unwrap_or_default()`
  - Check settings.json exists → error if not
  - Parse settings.json; bundle errors similarly
  - Dedup merge into existing allow list using `HashSet<String>`
  - Ensure `settings["permissions"]["allow"]` is set (create `permissions` key if missing)
  - Write via `serde_json::to_string_pretty(&settings)` + `"\n"`; bundle write errors as "Could not write settings.json: ..."
  - `let _ = fs::remove_file(local_path)` — silent
  - Return `{"status":"ok","promoted":[...],"already_present":N}`
- `fn run_impl(args: &Args) -> Result<Value, String>` — wraps `promote`, promotes the error-status case to Err so `run()` exits 1
- `fn run(args: Args)`

Uses existing `preserve_order` feature of `serde_json` to preserve non-permissions keys in insertion order.

### Task 8 — Write `tests/prime_check.rs` auto-upgrade cases

Add 8 more tests to `tests/prime_check.rs`:

1. `auto_upgrades_when_both_hashes_match` — set flow_version=0.0.1 + matching config_hash + matching setup_hash → `auto_upgraded: true`, framework echoed
2. `auto_upgrade_updates_version_in_file` — after call, re-read .flow.json, assert flow_version is now the current plugin version
3. `auto_upgrade_preserves_existing_fields` — preserve framework, skills, config_hash, setup_hash
4. `requires_reinit_when_config_hash_missing` — version mismatch + no config_hash → error "mismatch"
5. `requires_reinit_when_config_hash_mismatches` — zeros config_hash + valid setup_hash → error
6. `requires_reinit_when_setup_hash_missing` — valid config_hash + no setup_hash → error
7. `requires_reinit_when_setup_hash_mismatches` — valid config_hash + zeros setup_hash → error
8. `auto_upgrade_skills_preserved_with_order` — write a skills dict with a known key order, assert the order survives writeback (preserve_order check)

These tests require the test helper to compute the current Python config_hash and setup_hash. Options:
(a) Hardcode per-framework hashes in the test (requires updating tests whenever constants change — same drift risk as constants themselves)
(b) Compute hashes by shelling out to Python (`python3 -c "import sys; sys.path.insert(0, 'lib'); import importlib.util; ..."`) — reliable and always current
(c) Compute hashes via the Rust implementation itself from inside the test (but that's a self-test — if the Rust impl is wrong, the test still passes)

Use option (b). The test shells out to Python to get the canonical expected hashes, then writes a .flow.json containing those hashes, then runs the Rust binary and asserts it accepts them. This is a true round-trip parity check — if Python and Rust agree on the hash for the same inputs, auto-upgrade succeeds. Since `bin/ci` runs with the project virtualenv active and `lib/prime-setup.py` exists, the subprocess call works in CI.

- `fn read_flow_json(cwd: &Path) -> Option<serde_json::Value>` — reads `cwd/.flow.json`, parses, returns None on any I/O or parse error (matches Python `read_flow_json` from `flow_utils`)

### Task 11 — Implement `run_impl` + error messages + writeback

Add to `src/prime_check.rs`:

- `#[derive(clap::Args)] struct Args {}` — no args
- `fn run_impl(cwd: &Path, plugin_root: &Path) -> Result<Value, String>`:
  - Read `.flow.json` via `read_flow_json(cwd)`; if None → return `Ok(json!({"status":"error","message":"FLOW not initialized. Run /flow:flow-prime first."}))` (Python returns the error as JSON, not as an exception — Rust does the same)
  - Read plugin.json at `plugin_root/.claude-plugin/plugin.json`, extract `version`
  - Extract `flow_version` from init_data, filtering empty strings via `.and_then(|v| v.as_str()).filter(|s| !s.is_empty())`
  - If version mismatch:
    - Compute stored config_hash, stored setup_hash from init_data (string values, filter empty)
    - Compute plugin config_hash (with framework from init_data or empty string) and setup_hash
    - If both stored present AND both match plugin → auto-upgrade: set `init_data["flow_version"] = plugin_version`, write back with `serde_json::to_string(&init_data) + "\n"` (NOT to_string_pretty), return `{"status":"ok","framework":...,"auto_upgraded":true,"old_version":...,"new_version":...}`
    - Else → return `{"status":"error","message":"FLOW version mismatch: initialized for v<stored>, plugin is v<current>. Run /flow:flow-prime --reprime to upgrade (keeps current config), or /flow:flow-prime to reconfigure."}`
  - Version matches: extract framework, validate in set `{"rails","python","ios","go","rust"}`, return ok or error
- `fn run(args: Args)`:
  - Resolve `cwd = std::env::current_dir()`
  - Resolve `plugin_root = plugin_root().ok_or_else(...)`
  - Call `run_impl(&cwd, &plugin_root)`, print JSON, exit

Note: `run_impl` returns `Ok(Value)` even for user-facing error-JSON responses (matches Python behavior — the script always exits 0 and signals errors via the JSON `status` field). `Err` is reserved for infrastructure failures (plugin_root not found, etc.) that exit 1.

### NODE 1: Foundation & constraint map (depends on: none)

**Constraints discovered from reading the four Python scripts, prime-setup.py, and codebase patterns:**

**Runtime dependency map:**
- `lib/prime-check.py` -> imports `compute_config_hash`/`compute_setup_hash` from `lib/prime-setup.py` via `importlib` (dynamic sibling import)
- `lib/prime-project.py` -> imported by `lib/prime-setup.py` at line 542-543 (`_import_sibling("prime_project", "prime-project.py")`)
- `lib/detect-framework.py` -> no Python callers
- `lib/promote-permissions.py` -> no Python callers

**Cross-file references found:**
- 20 files reference the four scripts (verified via grep)
- Skill files (`flow-start`, `flow-prime`, `flow-learn`) invoke via `bin/flow <subcmd>` — automatically work via hybrid dispatcher once Rust subcommands land
- `tests/test_prime_setup.py` tests end-to-end via `_run_main` — will hit the modified subprocess path
- `tests/test_structural.py`, `tests/test_skill_contracts.py` have prose references only (docstrings, line 2410 checks `"promote-permissions" in step_text`) — these stay
- `docs/`, `CLAUDE.md`, `RELEASE-NOTES.md` — prose references only

**Hash function constraint:**
- `compute_config_hash(framework)` depends on `UNIVERSAL_ALLOW` (48 entries), `FLOW_DENY` (10 entries), `EXCLUDE_ENTRIES` (5 entries) defined in `prime-setup.py`. Hash is sha256 of canonical-sorted JSON, truncated to 12 hex chars.
- `compute_setup_hash()` is sha256 of `prime-setup.py` file bytes, truncated to 12 hex chars. Trivial to port (no logic, just file I/O).
- Since prime-setup.py is out of scope, the Rust port MUST duplicate the constants. Drift is a correctness bug. Mitigation: a Python contract test that reads both Python constants and the Rust source and asserts equality.

**Precedent for Python-to-Rust handoff in prime-setup.py:**
Lines 545-556 already show the pattern: `create-dependencies` was previously an import, now it's a subprocess call to `bin/flow create-dependencies`. This is the template for the prime-project dependency break.

**`frameworks_dir()` already exists in Rust** (`src/utils.rs:80`) as `plugin_root().map(|r| r.join("frameworks"))`.

**Tests pattern:**
- Rust integration tests go in `tests/<name>.rs`, use `env!("CARGO_BIN_EXE_flow-rs")` as the binary, use `tempfile::tempdir()`, use `Command::output()` (never `.status()` — see `tests/upgrade_check.rs`, `tests/check_phase.rs`)
- Coverage requirement: "Each tests/test_*.py corresponds to a lib/*.py with 100% coverage" — must maintain coverage parity

**Key port risks (rust-port-parity.md cross-check):**
1. **Constant duplication drift** — mitigated by contract test (Node 6)
2. **compute_config_hash JSON key order** — Python `json.dumps(..., sort_keys=True)` -> Rust must use BTreeMap or sort manually before serialize_json
3. **compute_setup_hash file path resolution** — Python uses `Path(__file__).resolve()`; Rust must resolve `plugin_root().join("lib/prime-setup.py")`
4. **prime-project subprocess cwd** — Python `prime()` takes `project_root` as arg and writes to `project_root/CLAUDE.md`. No subprocess in Python side. Rust port writes directly.
5. **promote-permissions 2-space indent** — Python `json.dump(..., indent=2)` followed by `f.write("\n")`. Rust must use `serde_json::to_string_pretty` (2-space default) + trailing newline.
6. **promote-permissions key ordering** — `serde_json::Value` with `preserve_order` feature already enabled in Cargo.toml
7. **detect-framework glob matching** — Python `Path.glob(pattern)`. Actual detect.json patterns: `Gemfile`, `Cargo.toml`, `go.mod`, `pyproject.toml`, `setup.py`, `requirements.txt`, `*.xcodeproj`. No `glob` crate needed — tiny inline matcher suffices.
8. **prime-check flow_version == None vs missing** — Python uses `dict.get()` with None check. Rust must use `.and_then(|v| v.as_str()).filter(|s| !s.is_empty())` per rust-port-parity empty-string rule.
9. **prime-project marker position** — Python checks `existing_content[end_index] == "\n"` to swallow trailing newline. Rust port must handle byte-safe indexing (ASCII markers so byte indexing is safe, but document invariant).
10. **clap required=True mutually-exclusive groups** — promote-permissions has `--worktree-path required=True`, a single required arg not a group. detect-framework takes positional. prime-project takes positional + `--framework`. prime-check takes no args. None have mutually exclusive groups, so ArgGroup rule is N/A.

**Key finding:** All detect.json patterns are either literal filenames or simple `*.ext`. No `glob` crate dependency needed — implement inline.

Quality: 9/10 — constraint map complete, glob pattern set is trivially tiny, no external crate needed.

### PARALLEL BRANCH A — NODE 2: Port detect-framework

**Plan:**
- `src/detect_framework.rs` with pure functions `detect(project, fw_dir)`, `available_frameworks(fw_dir)`, `run_impl(args) -> Result<Value, String>`, `run(args)` exit wrapper

- Key finding: glob set in detect.json is tiny and easily covered without a crate dep
- Quality: 9/10 — straightforward port, no tricky edges

**Matcher logic:**
```rust
fn matches_glob(dir: &Path, pattern: &str) -> bool {
    if let Some(stripped) = pattern.strip_prefix("*.") {
        match std::fs::read_dir(dir) {
            Ok(entries) => entries.filter_map(|e| e.ok())
                .any(|e| e.file_name().to_string_lossy().ends_with(&format!(".{}", stripped))),
            Err(_) => false,
        }
    } else {
        dir.join(pattern).exists()
    }
}
```

### NODE 5: Port prime-check with hash functions

**Plan:**
- `src/prime_check.rs` with:
  - `const UNIVERSAL_ALLOW: &[&str] = &[...]` (48 entries, duplicated from prime-setup.py)
  - `const FLOW_DENY: &[&str] = &[...]` (10 entries)
  - `const EXCLUDE_ENTRIES: &[&str] = &[...]` (5 entries)
  - `fn compute_config_hash(framework: &str, fw_dir: &Path) -> Result<String, String>`
  - `fn compute_setup_hash(plugin_root: &Path) -> Result<String, String>`
  - `fn run_impl(cwd: &Path, plugin_root: &Path) -> Result<Value, String>`
  - `fn run()` wrapper
- **Custom serde_json Formatter** (`PythonDefaultFormatter`) that emits `, ` and `: ` separators to match `json.dumps(..., sort_keys=True)` output byte-for-byte
- **Sort-key parity** via `BTreeMap<String, Value>` at top level (alphabetical: allow, defaultMode, deny, exclude)
- **Setup hash** = `sha256(fs::read(plugin_root/"lib/prime-setup.py"))` -> hex -> truncate 12
- **Helper**: `read_flow_json(cwd: &Path) -> Option<Value>` — parses .flow.json, returns None on any I/O or parse error (matches Python)
- **Error message strings** must match Python exactly: "FLOW not initialized. Run /flow:flow-prime first.", "FLOW version mismatch: initialized for v<v>, plugin is v<v>. Run /flow:flow-prime --reprime to upgrade (keeps current config), or /flow:flow-prime to reconfigure.", "Missing framework in .flow.json. Run /flow:flow-prime to configure."
- **Empty-string vs missing-key** — `flow_version` must filter `Some("")` as falsy: `.and_then(|v| v.as_str()).filter(|s| !s.is_empty())`
- **Auto-upgrade writeback**: preserve ALL existing keys in .flow.json (skills, config_hash, setup_hash, etc.), only flip flow_version. Python uses `init_data["flow_version"] = plugin_version; flow_json.write_text(json.dumps(init_data) + "\n")` — no indent. Rust must match: `serde_json::to_string(&data)` + `"\n"`.
- `tests/prime_check.rs` with 15 integration tests mirroring Python (missing .flow.json, wrong version, missing framework, happy path rails/python/go, 8 auto-upgrade branches)
- Quality: 8/10 — custom Formatter is the subtle part; hash parity is correctness-critical

**Custom Formatter draft:**
```rust
use serde_json::ser::Formatter;
use std::io;

struct PythonDefaultFormatter;

impl Formatter for PythonDefaultFormatter {
    fn begin_object_key<W>(&mut self, writer: &mut W, first: bool) -> io::Result<()>
    where W: ?Sized + io::Write {
        if first { Ok(()) } else { writer.write_all(b", ") }
    }
    fn begin_object_value<W>(&mut self, writer: &mut W) -> io::Result<()>
    where W: ?Sized + io::Write {
        writer.write_all(b": ")
    }
    fn begin_array_value<W>(&mut self, writer: &mut W, first: bool) -> io::Result<()>
    where W: ?Sized + io::Write {
        if first { Ok(()) } else { writer.write_all(b", ") }
    }
}
```

======================================================================
DAG SYNTHESIS
======================================================================

# Port Prime Utilities to Rust — Synthesis

## Context

Issue #786 (Phase 7 of the Python-to-Rust port) requires porting four
prime-adjacent utilities (prime-check, prime-project, detect-framework,
promote-permissions) to Rust subcommands on the flow-rs binary. Tests
from Python (test_prime_check.py 264 lines, test_prime_project.py
124 lines, test_promote_permissions.py 315 lines) must be ported to
Rust integration tests. Python files must be deleted. bin/ci must pass.
prime-setup.py is explicitly out of scope but has a runtime dependency
on prime-project.py that must be severed to enable deletion.

## Key Findings

1. Only ONE of the four Python files has a Python caller: prime-setup.py
   imports prime-project.py at line 542-543. The other three have no
   Python importers, so deletion is unblocked after Rust ports land.

2. prime-check.py has a LOGICAL dependency (not an import) on
   prime-setup.py via `compute_config_hash(framework)` and
   `compute_setup_hash()`. These functions use three constants
   (UNIVERSAL_ALLOW, FLOW_DENY, EXCLUDE_ENTRIES) defined in
   prime-setup.py. The Rust port must duplicate these constants.

3. The codebase already has a precedent for breaking Python runtime
   deps when porting: prime-setup.py lines 545-556 show
   create-dependencies being called via subprocess to bin/flow. The
   prime-project dependency is fixed with the identical pattern.

4. detect_globs in frameworks/*/detect.json use only literal filenames
   and one `*.ext` wildcard — no `glob` crate dependency needed.

5. Python's `json.dumps(sort_keys=True)` default uses `(", ", ": ")`
   separators. Rust's `serde_json::to_string` uses `(",", ":")`. This
   produces DIFFERENT hash bytes. Fix: custom serde_json Formatter.

6. test_detect_framework.py is omitted from the AC test-port list but
   must be ported — otherwise deleting lib/detect-framework.py leaves
   a dangling test.

## Exploration

Files read:
- lib/prime-check.py (132 lines) — version gate + auto-upgrade
- lib/prime-project.py (107 lines) — CLAUDE.md marker insertion
- lib/detect-framework.py (111 lines) — data-driven framework detection
- lib/promote-permissions.py (93 lines) — merge settings.local into settings
- lib/prime-setup.py (587 lines, relevant sections only) — hash functions,
  prime-project import site, create-dependencies subprocess precedent
- tests/test_prime_check.py (264 lines) — 15 test cases
- tests/test_prime_project.py (124 lines) — 11 test cases
- tests/test_detect_framework.py (183 lines) — 17 test cases
- tests/test_promote_permissions.py (315 lines) — 18 test cases
- tests/test_prime_setup.py (relevant sections, lines 1085-1135) —
  prime_project integration
- tests/test_log_bridge.py — bridge pattern precedent
- src/utils.rs (first 150 lines) — plugin_root, frameworks_dir,
  read_version helpers already exist
- src/lib.rs, src/main.rs — module registration and subcommand dispatch
- src/commands/init_state.rs (pattern reference)
- tests/upgrade_check.rs (Rust integration test pattern reference)
- tests/check_phase.rs (Rust integration test pattern reference)
- bin/flow (dispatcher, hybrid Rust-first pattern)

Cross-file references grep confirmed 20 files mention the four scripts;
only tests/ and lib/prime-setup.py are functional dependents. Skills,
docs, CLAUDE.md, RELEASE-NOTES are prose references and do not need
code edits.

## Risks

1. **Hash byte-parity risk**: Python `json.dumps(canonical, sort_keys=True)`
   produces output with `, ` and `: ` separators. Default Rust serde_json
   emits `,` and `:`. Custom PythonDefaultFormatter required. Verified by
   end-to-end parity test in Node 6 (Rust prime-check auto-upgrades a
   .flow.json with Python-computed hashes).

2. **Constant drift risk**: UNIVERSAL_ALLOW/FLOW_DENY/EXCLUDE_ENTRIES
   duplicated in both Python (prime-setup.py) and Rust (prime_check.rs).
   Any edit to Python constants invalidates the Rust hash. Mitigated by
   static parity test (Node 6) that reads both files and asserts equal
   entries.

3. **test_prime_setup.py subprocess dependency**: After Node 8 edit,
   test_prime_setup.py's prime_project assertions call bin/flow
   prime-project via subprocess. CI must build the Rust binary BEFORE
   running pytest — verified during Node 12.

4. **Write-error test fixtures on Rust**: Python tests use monkeypatch
   to make open() fail. Rust equivalent uses chmod 444 on the target
   file. chmod behavior differs between macOS and Linux for some edge
   cases (root filesystem permissions), but for regular user temp files
   both platforms honor the mode. Low risk.

5. **Empty-string flow_version**: Python `if init_data.get(...) != version`
   treats "" as equal only if version is "". Rust must filter empty
   strings explicitly — missed this would silently auto-upgrade when
   flow_version is "".

6. **Auto-upgrade writeback format**: Python uses
   `json.dumps(init_data) + "\n"` — no indent, no key sorting. Rust
   port must match. Using preserve_order feature (already enabled) plus
   `serde_json::to_string` (not to_string_pretty) preserves insertion
   order which for an already-parsed dict matches the input order.

7. **Target path for compute_setup_hash**: Python reads
   `Path(__file__).resolve()` — i.e., prime-setup.py itself. Rust
   must compute `plugin_root().join("lib/prime-setup.py")`, which is
   the same file via a different path resolution strategy. If
   plugin_root() returns None (unusual), the hash computation fails
   gracefully.

8. **glob matcher correctness**: Python `Path.glob("*.xcodeproj")`
   matches entries where `name.endswith(".xcodeproj")`. Rust inline
   matcher must use `file_name().ends_with(".xcodeproj")` — NOT
   `entry.path().extension()` which would fail on directories named
   "Foo.xcodeproj" (they're dirs, no extension by Path semantics).
   Verified against test_detects_ios_when_xcodeproj_exists which
   creates `project/MyApp.xcodeproj` as a DIRECTORY.

## Approach

Port each utility as an independent Rust module with:
1. Pure logic function (unit-testable)
2. `run_impl(args) -> Result<Value, String>` (CLI-testable)
3. `run(args)` exit wrapper

Register all four as clap subcommands in src/main.rs. Delete the Python
files after Rust parity is proven. Edit prime-setup.py to subprocess-call
prime-project (matching create-dependencies precedent). Add parity tests
(static constant comparison + end-to-end hash round-trip). Add tombstone
tests to prevent resurrection via merge.

TDD order per port: write Rust integration tests first (against
non-existent modules — tests fail to compile), then implement the
modules, then watch tests pass.

## Dependency Graph

| Task | Type | Depends On |
|------|------|------------|
| 1. Write detect_framework tests | test | — |
| 2. Implement src/detect_framework.rs | implement | 1 |
| 3. Write promote_permissions tests | test | — |
| 4. Implement src/promote_permissions.rs | implement | 3 |
| 5. Write prime_project tests | test | — |
| 6. Implement src/prime_project.rs | implement | 5 |
| 7. Write prime_check tests (including auto-upgrade) | test | — |
| 8. Implement src/prime_check.rs constants+hash funcs | implement | 7 |
| 9. Implement src/prime_check.rs run_impl + PythonDefaultFormatter | implement | 8 |
| 10. Wire 4 modules into src/lib.rs + src/main.rs | implement | 2,4,6,9 |
| 11. cargo build --release | validate | 10 |
| 12. Write constant parity test (tests/test_prime_port_parity.py) | test | 10 |
| 13. Edit lib/prime-setup.py to subprocess-call prime-project | implement | 10,12 |
| 14. Audit tests/test_prime_setup.py for regressions | validate | 13 |
| 15. Delete 4 lib/*.py files | implement | 13,14 |
| 16. Delete 4 tests/test_*.py files | implement | 15 |
| 17. Add 8 tombstone tests in tests/test_structural.py | test | 15,16 |
| 18. Run bin/ci | validate | 17 |
| 19. Cross-check rust-port-parity.md | validate | 18 |

Parallelizable sets:
- {1,3,5,7} — four independent test-writing tasks
- {2,4,6,8} — four independent implementation tasks (each after its tests)
- {13,14} — parallel audit during prime-setup edit

## Tasks

1. **Write detect_framework integration tests** — create tests/detect_framework.rs
   with 17 test cases mirroring tests/test_detect_framework.py. Use
   tempfile::tempdir + env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR")/frameworks for fixtures.
   Use Command::output() for every subprocess call. Tests fail with
   "module not found" at this stage. TDD: tests before impl.

2. **Implement src/detect_framework.rs** — add detect(), available_frameworks(),
   inline glob matcher, run_impl(), run(). Use frameworks_dir() from utils.
   Sort detect.json files to match Python behavior. Tests now pass.

3. **Write promote_permissions integration tests** — create tests/promote_permissions.rs
   with 18 test cases. Write-error test: chmod 0o444 on settings.json.
   Delete-silent test: chmod 0o555 on .claude dir. Use Command::output().

4. **Implement src/promote_permissions.rs** — add promote() pure fn,
   run_impl(), run(). Use IndexMap via preserve_order. Write with
   to_string_pretty + trailing newline. Silent remove_file. Tests pass.

5. **Write prime_project integration tests** — create tests/prime_project.rs
   with 11 test cases. Use env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR")/frameworks for
   real priming.md fixtures (rails and python).

6. **Implement src/prime_project.rs** — add prime() pure fn with
   marker byte-offset manipulation (documented ASCII-safe), run_impl(),
   run(). Tests pass.

7. **Write prime_check integration tests** — create tests/prime_check.rs
   with 15 test cases covering: missing .flow.json, version mismatch
   (no hash, mismatched config_hash, missing setup_hash, mismatched
   setup_hash), missing framework, invalid framework, happy-path
   rails/python/go, auto-upgrade (rails/python/go), auto-upgrade writes
   file, auto-upgrade preserves skills field. Fixture requires plugin
   root discovery — use env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR") as the plugin_root
   override (via CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT env var) so the test can resolve
   the real plugin.json for version and the real frameworks/ dir.

8. **Implement src/prime_check.rs constants + hash functions** — define
   UNIVERSAL_ALLOW, FLOW_DENY, EXCLUDE_ENTRIES (duplicated from
   prime-setup.py). Implement PythonDefaultFormatter to emit
   ", " and ": " separators. Implement compute_config_hash (uses
   BTreeMap for top-level sort_keys parity). Implement compute_setup_hash
   (sha256 of prime-setup.py bytes, hex, truncate 12).

9. **Implement src/prime_check.rs run_impl + writeback** — read
   .flow.json via local helper, read plugin.json, compare versions,
   auto-upgrade branch with exact Python error message strings,
   writeback using serde_json::to_string + "\n" (no indent, preserve
   insertion order). Integration tests from task 7 now pass.

10. **Wire into src/lib.rs + src/main.rs** — register 4 modules and
    4 clap subcommands (PrimeCheck no-arg; PrimeProject positional
    project_root + --framework; DetectFramework positional;
    PromotePermissions --worktree-path). Add match arms dispatching
    to each module's run(). Verify with cargo build --release.

11. **Run cargo build --release** — confirm no compile errors,
    binary at target/release/flow-rs supports all 4 new subcommands.

12. **Write constant parity test** — create
    tests/test_prime_port_parity.py with two layers:
    (a) Static: read lib/prime-setup.py, import UNIVERSAL_ALLOW/
        FLOW_DENY/EXCLUDE_ENTRIES via importlib; read src/prime_check.rs
        as text, extract the three const arrays via regex, compare
        entry-by-entry; fail with a detailed diff on mismatch.
    (b) End-to-end: for each framework (rails, python, ios, go, rust),
        compute Python hash, write a temp .flow.json with old_version
        and the Python hash, invoke bin/flow prime-check, assert
        response is status=ok + auto_upgraded=true. Proves Rust
        computes identical hashes to Python.

13. **Edit lib/prime-setup.py prime-project dependency** — replace
    lines 542-543 (the _import_sibling + prime() call) with a subprocess
    call to bin/flow prime-project, following the exact pattern at
    lines 545-556 (create-dependencies precedent). Preserve error
    handling semantics: on any exception, prime_result becomes
    {"status": "error", "message": str(e)}.

14. **Audit tests/test_prime_setup.py** — run the file in isolation
    (bin/test tests/test_prime_setup.py) after task 13. Verify
    test_cli_primes_project_claude_md and
    test_cli_prime_project_error_does_not_block_success still pass.
    Fix any regression caused by the in-process -> subprocess transition.
    Confirm bin/ci builds Rust binary before pytest (check bin/ci order).

15. **Delete Python lib files** — delete lib/prime-check.py,
    lib/prime-project.py, lib/detect-framework.py, lib/promote-permissions.py
    via Bash rm (or prefer git rm via /flow:flow-commit).

16. **Delete Python test files** — delete tests/test_prime_check.py,
    tests/test_prime_project.py, tests/test_detect_framework.py,
    tests/test_promote_permissions.py. The Rust tests from tasks 1/3/5/7
    now provide coverage.

17. **Add tombstone tests** — add 8 tombstone tests to
    tests/test_structural.py (or a new tests/test_port_tombstones.py
    if structural gets crowded), following the tombstone-tests.md
    pattern: test function name test_tombstone_lib_<name>_removed
    with docstring referencing PR #<num> and assertion that the
    path does not exist.

18. **Run bin/ci** — full test suite (cargo build + cargo test +
    pytest + ruff). Must be green. Fix any failures at the source
    (never suppress lint, never skip test).

19. **Cross-check rust-port-parity.md** — walk the checklist for each
    of the 4 Rust ports: run_impl extraction OK, Command::output() in
    tests OK, subprocess CWD N/A (no subprocs in these), upfront guards
    placement in run_impl, empty-string falsy handling for flow_version,
    JSON key ordering strategy, sentinel return docs, string slicing
    ASCII invariant. Document any exceptions inline.

## Contract Test Atomicity

Task 13 (edit prime-setup.py) removes the in-process import path.
test_prime_setup.py tests `test_cli_primes_project_claude_md` and
`test_cli_prime_project_error_does_not_block_success` depend on the
new subprocess path. These must be verified in the same commit as
task 13 to avoid a red CI intermediate state. Task 14 is the
validation.

Tasks 15, 16 (deletions) are safe to land separately from task 13
IF the Rust binary is available (it is, after task 11). But to avoid
any window where Rust is built but Python callers still exist, tasks
13 + 15 + 16 should land together.

Tasks 17 (tombstones) must land AFTER tasks 15/16 — tombstones assert
files don't exist, so they fail if filed before deletion.

## Confidence: 92%

vs. Vanilla Claude (what linear reasoning would have missed):
  - The prime-setup.py hash-function dependency would likely be
    discovered mid-implementation, forcing a mid-plan rewrite
    ("wait, prime-check needs constants from prime-setup...").
  - The Python `json.dumps` default separator `(", ", ": ")` vs
    Rust `serde_json::to_string` `(",", ":")` byte mismatch is a
    silent hash bug — would have shipped broken without explicit
    trace through the hash round-trip logic.
  - The prime-setup.py create-dependencies precedent at lines 545-556
    is the blueprint for breaking the prime-project import dep; linear
    reasoning might propose a Python bridge file instead, missing the
    cleaner in-place precedent.
  - test_detect_framework.py is omitted from the AC test-port list —
    linear reasoning would faithfully skip it and then be confused
    when deleting lib/detect-framework.py leaves a broken test import.
  - Tombstone tests for 8 deletions is easy to forget; the DAG's
    dedicated Node 11 surfaces this as an explicit deliverable.
======================================================================
```
````

items.title: Fix multiprocessing with forkserver in python 3.13.13 and 3.14.4
items.body:
Python 3.15.0a8 introduced a change to `forkserver` spawn mode of `multiprocessing`, and that change was also back-ported to python 3.13.13 and 3.14.4.

See: https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/148194
    
In the modified version, the `sys.argv` elements passed to the `forkserver` process have been moved from the command string (that follows `-c` switch) itself into additional command-line arguments.
    
This breaks our current argument-matching implementation in `multiprocessing` run-time hook, which assumes that the command string is the last passed argument. In addition, the command string itself changed a bit (there is an added `import sys; ` at the beginning).
    
Therefore, revise the argument matching to find the index of `-c` argument, and use the following argument (if available) as the command string to match. Add the pattern for the new forkserver command to ensure match under these new python versions.

items.title: Port sprinkler API from Raspberry Pi to XIAO ESP32-C3 (PlatformIO)
items.body:
Replaces the Python/FastAPI server and Docker setup with a C++ Arduino-framework project targeting the Seeed XIAO ESP32-C3. Serves the same POST /api/schedule endpoint over WiFi using ESPAsyncWebServer and ArduinoJson, controlling 3 relay zones via GPIO. Includes concise PlatformIO setup instructions in README.md.

https://claude.ai/code/session_017zBp3U21HPuWS5LHoFE8hx

items.title: Conversion to Python Backend
items.body:
# Overview
This PR converts the existing Node backend to Python.  Additionally, moves all (current) validations from the frontend to the backend and thus removes the need to send up answers.  Removing the dictionary from the frontend, as well.

## Advisories
### No Dictionary
There are no dictionary checks as of this moment - we've pulled them out of the frontend.  We should discuss one of the many available strategies to re-implement this, but for now it has been completely removed until we pick one.

### Port Change
The Python backend chosen for the API is known as [FastAPI](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/).  By default, FastAPI serves the application on port `8000`.  This is a change from Node's Express framework, which serves by default on port `3000`.

## Versions
For the moment, the versions selected for the backends are:
- Python: `3.14`
- Postgres: `17`

# Technical Change Details
## Criteria Strategy
I have provided a base set of classes that pave the way for re-usable, parameterizable Criteria.  I've started with a few basics like "Ends With," "Begins With," "Double Letter," and Length-related ones.  The important thing to realize is that these are re-usable and unit-testable.  As a convenience, they also provide default labels, but these can be overridden easily if desired.

There is an abstract `Criterion` object that glues everything together.  Any implementation of a criterion that extends this class will automatically work in the new architecture for API responses and well as configuration, etc.

## Configuration Strategy
Now that we are no longer generating entire answer sets, we still need a way to create new puzzles whenever we'd like.  The file `src/lib/Puzzle/Config.py` now does that.  For now, this exists as a Python dictionary keyed by the date, and the values are the 3 implemented Criteria objects for that day's Puzzle.

Example `Config.py` structure:
```python
puzzle_config = {
    "2026-03-27": [Common.EndsWithLetter("r"), Length.AtLeastLength(8), Common.HasDoubleLetters()],
    "2026-03-23": [Common.StartsWithLetter("b"), Common.EndsWithLetter("t"), Length.ExactLength(7)],
}
```

**NOTE**: These Puzzle IDs are just string values... They _look_ like dates, but they are not required to be.  For the moment, there is no date-based logic built in to any of this.

## Endpoints
### GET Current Puzzle
Shape: `GET /api/puzzles/current`

Where `puzzleData.json` used to be loaded, we now replace that with an API call that simply returns the first puzzle that exists in the Config object.  Assuming the file is maintained from the bottom-up, the first record will always be the "latest" and there is no need to worry about it falling out of date, etc (again, there is no actual date logic right now).  Although there is no longer any "region data" sent back to the browser, the structure of this new endpoint's response is designed to fit what the `puzzleData.json` file was previously providing in terms of Puzzle ID and region labels.

Example:
```sh
# GET Current Puzzle:
GET /api/puzzles/current

# Server response:
{
  "id": "2026-03-27",
  "labels": {
    "A": "Ends with R",
    "B": "At least 8 letters",
    "C": "Has double letters"
  }
}
```

### GET Word and Region info by Puzzle ID
Shape: `GET /api/puzzles/{puzzleId}/words/{word}`

This request asks for details about a word, but specifically within the context of the given Puzzle.  The response gives details about which Region ID it belongs to (based on the Puzzle's config), as well as some extra information such as the normalized word that was used in the backend, and the Region labels it matched that led to the calculation.  All this other data is nice-to-have, but the only data actually used from this response right now in the frontend is just the `region_id`.

Example:
```sh
# GET word "PuPPeTRy" for puzzle 2026-03-27
GET /api/puzzles/2026-03-27/words/PuPPeTRy

# Server response:
{
  "id": "puppetry",
  "input": "PuPPeTRy",
  "puzzle_id": "2026-03-27",
  "region_id": 6,
  "region_matches": [
    "At least 8 letters",
    "Has double letters"
  ]
}
```

### POST a Solution
Shape: `POST /api/puzzles/{puzzleId}/solutions`

This replaces the previous `/api/solves` endpoint, as-is.  However, we now pass in the Puzzle ID in the URL, so it does not need to be a body parameter.  Otherwise, it is a drop-in replacement for what was there previously.

Example:
```sh
# Submit a solution for Puzzle 2026-03-27
POST /api/puzzles/2026-03-27/solutions
{
  "solveTimeSeconds": "60"
}

# Server response
{
  "success": true
}
```
#### NOTICE: No Validations!
This endpoint needs to be augmented to accept all seven words so that the client can "prove" to the backend that a full solution has been found.  By providing the Puzzle ID and all seven words, the backend can verify for itself if the solution is legitimate.

This PR does not include that logic, but I have another PR behind this that paves the way for it once/if this one is merged.

### GET stats for Puzzle
Shape `GET /api/puzzles/{puzzleId}/stats`

This is a drop-in replacement for the previous `GET /api/stats/{puzzleId}` endpoint with no changes in logic or usage.

Example:
```sh
# GET stats for Puzzle ID 2026-03-27
GET /api/puzzles/2026-03-27/stats

# Server response
{
  "playersSolved": 4,
  "averageTime": 121
}
```

### GET Puzzle by ID
Shape: `GET /api/puzzles/{puzzleId}`

Not currently used, but trivial to implement and allows easy inspection of previous puzzles (also paving the way for pulling histories, dealing with time changeovers, etc).  This can be safely ignored for now but, like I said, it was trivial to implement.

Example:
```sh
# GET puzzle ID 2026-03-23
GET /api/puzzles/2026-03-23

# Server response:
{
  "id": "2026-03-23",
  "labels": {
    "A": "Must begin with the letter b",
    "B": "Ends with T",
    "C": "Is 7 letters"
  }
}
```

# Commit Log Summaries
## Rewritten backend using Python
- FastAPI and dependencies
- Initial Route
- Static File Mount
- Containerization
- Removed `puzzleData.json` file
- Moved `dictionary.txt`

## Baseline Domain logic
- Puzzle models, config, loaders
- Criteria framework
- Basic routes

## Adding Postgres Connection to Python Backend
- Attaching Pool initialization to FastAPI "Lifespan" hooks
- Currently doing everything inline within `server.py`
- Writing Solves to DB; associated endpoint
- Exposing Stats endpoint

## Updating frontend script to use new backend features
- Using new endpoints for puzzle loading, region discovery, and solution submission
- Shockingly, the region IDs mapped correctly (this should be re-verified)
- Converting some sync calls to async in order to support new API call
- Misc. deletions


# TODO
- [ ] As of this PR, there are no more dictionary checks.  This logic has a place in the backend now, but it has not been implemented (the current check just returns `True` for all inputs for the moment).  This should be addressed in a follow-up PR.
- [ ] Remove the Node server, `node_modules`, and other Node/NPM -related code if the Python server approach will be accepted.

items.title: Convert MultiFX Processor to Windows-only (NI USB-6009 + named pipe)
items.body:
The project was ~80% Windows-compatible but had blocking Linux remnants: POSIX serial I/O in the C engine, a broken `ni6009_feeder.py` (undefined `PIPE_NAME`, missing `win32pipe`/`win32file` imports, wrong exception type), and a Linux-pipe-based `audio_rpi/ni6009_feeder.py`. This PR completes the Windows conversion.

## C Audio Engine (`audio_rpi/`)

- **`src/serial_input.c`** — Full rewrite of POSIX serial (`termios`, `unistd`, `fcntl`) to Windows COM port API:
  - `serial_autodetect()` scans `COM1`–`COM32` via `CreateFileA()`
  - `serial_open()` uses `CreateFile` + `GetCommState`/`SetCommState` (8N1, configurable baud) + `SetCommTimeouts` + `PurgeComm`
  - Internal I/O uses `ReadFile()` with `DWORD`-typed lengths (no unsafe int casts)
  - Handle-table pattern (`int fd → HANDLE[MAX_SERIAL_PORTS]`) avoids truncating 64-bit `HANDLE` to `int`; bounds-checked in `serial_close` and `serial_read_packet`
- **`include/serial_input.h`** — Updated comments from `/dev/ttyUSB*` to `COM1–COM32`

## Python Feeder (`ni6009_feeder.py`)

Both copies (in `Interfaz/` and `audio_rpi/`) now use Windows named pipe `\\.\pipe\ni6009`:

```python
import win32pipe, win32file, pywintypes

PIPE_NAME = r"\\.\pipe\ni6009"

def open_pipe():
    pipe = win32pipe.CreateNamedPipe(PIPE_NAME, win32pipe.PIPE_ACCESS_OUTBOUND, ...)
    win32pipe.ConnectNamedPipe(pipe, None)
    return pipe

# Write loop: catch Win32 errors, not POSIX BrokenPipeError
try:
    win32file.WriteFile(pipe_handle, packet)
except pywintypes.error:
    break
```

`signal.SIGTERM` (unreliable on Windows) replaced with `signal.SIGBREAK`.

## Housekeeping

- `requirements.txt` — Added `pywin32`; updated setup comments to Windows venv activation (`venv\Scripts\activate`, `python` not `python3`)
- `README.md` — Rewritten for Windows: prerequisites (MinGW, PortAudio, NI-DAQmx driver), `run_all.bat` usage, SIM_MODE reference table
- `run_all.sh` — Deleted (superseded by `run_all.bat`)

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

## Overview
Convert the MultiFX Processor from a Linux/Unix-focused project to a Windows-only system while preserving the core functionality: NI USB-6009 audio input via nidaqmx library and socket communication for phone app control.

## Current State Analysis
The project is already 80% Windows-compatible:
- Audio engine uses PortAudio (cross-platform)
- Main code has Windows headers (#include <windows.h>)
- Named pipe support exists (CreateFileA, ReadFile)
- run_all.bat exists for Windows process management

**Critical Issue:** `socket_server.c` uses Unix domain sockets (`AF_UNIX`) which don't exist on Windows.

## Required Changes

### 1. Socket Communication (socket_server.c)
**Current (Unix):**
- Unix domain sockets at `/tmp/audio_socket`
- `AF_UNIX` + `SOCK_STREAM`
- Non-blocking mode with fcntl

**Target (Windows):**
- TCP sockets on localhost (127.0.0.1:54321)
- Winsock2 API (WS2_32)
- Non-blocking with WSAAsyncSelect or ioctlsocket

### 2. Named Pipe Paths
- Update Python feeder: `/tmp/ni6009_pipe` → `\\.\pipe\ni6009`
- Update C engine path references
- Ensure Windows pipe creation/open semantics work correctly

### 3. Platform-Specific Build
- Remove ALSA/Unix-specific includes
- Keep PortAudio for audio output (Windows-compatible)
- Document Windows build process (Visual Studio or MinGW)

### 4. Process Management
- Update run_all.bat to use proper Windows process spawning
- Replace bash shell syntax with batch file syntax
- Ensure proper cleanup on Ctrl+C

### 5. Documentation & Configuration
- Update README.md for Windows setup
- Document nidaqmx Windows installation
- Provide Visual Studio project or Makefile.windows

## Acceptance Criteria
- ✅ socket_server.c compiles and works on Windows (TCP sockets)
- ✅ ni6009_feeder.py uses Windows named pipes
- ✅ Named pipe communication works between Python feeder and C engine
- ✅ TCP socket communication works between C engine and Python GUI
- ✅ Python GUI receives effect data over TCP
- ✅ Phone app can connect to TCP server on port 54321
- ✅ Audio effects processing runs without Linux-specific code
- ✅ No Unix domain sockets or /tmp dependencies
- ✅ README updated with Windows build/run instructions

## Implementation Notes
- Prioritize stability over optimization
- Use standard Windows APIs (avoid third-party IPC libraries)
- Maintain binary protocol compatibility (packet format unchanged)
- Keep code structure similar to minimize risk

## Project Analysis & Strategic Recommendations

Based on my investigation, your **ProyectoFinal** is a **MultiFX Audio Processor** with these components:

### Current Architecture:
1. **Python GUI** (PyQt6) - Effect control & visualization
2. **C Audio Engine** - Real-time DSP (effects: Overdrive, Wah, Delay, Chorus, Flanger, Phaser, PitchShifter, Reverb)
3. **NI USB-6009 Feeder** (Python) - Reads analog audio via nidaqmx → streams to C engine via named pipe
4. **IPC Communication**:
   - **Pipe** (Linux: `/tmp/ni6009_pipe` / Windows: `\\.\pipe\ni6009`) - Audio data (NI → C engine)
   - **Socket** (Linux: Unix domain / Windows: could be TCP) - Control JSON (Python GUI ↔ C engine)
   - **Phone App** - Mobile control via network sockets

### Your Request:
Convert from **Linux/Unix context** to **Windows-only**, keeping only:

---

## Difficulty Spikes & Recommendations

### **APPROACH A: "Minimal Windows Port" ✅ RECOMMENDED**

  - `main.c` uses `#include <windows.h>` and **PortAudio** (cross-platform)
  - `run_all.bat` exists for Windows process management
  - Named pipe uses Windows API (`CreateFileA`, `ReadFile`)
  - `socket_server.c` is **Linux-only** → must be rewritten for Windows TCP

**Difficulty: MEDIUM** (~4-6 hours)

items.title: fix(install): port shell-level GPU swap from install.sh to split installers
items.body:
## Summary

High-severity regression: the split installers (`install-server.sh`,
`install-client.sh`, `install-both.sh`) deleted the 40-line shell-level
`onnxruntime` -> `onnxruntime-gpu` swap when they were extracted from
`install.sh`, replacing it with the comment `# GPU runtime handled by
'quarry install' below`.  That comment was wrong: the Python-side
`ensure_gpu_runtime()` in `src/quarry/service.py` reports
`"onnxruntime-gpu installed"` (rc=0) but the GPU wheel is NOT present
in the tool venv afterward under real conditions.  Every NVIDIA user
who ran the one-liner silently ended up on `CPUExecutionProvider` with
a CPU-only `onnxruntime` wheel in the tool venv — a 10x+ throughput
regression invisible to `make check`.

Verified directly on a fresh okinos install (RTX 5080, driver
570.211.01, punt-quarry 1.12.1, Python 3.13.2):

- Before: `site-packages/onnxruntime-1.24.4.dist-info` only,
  `get_available_providers()` = `['AzureExecutionProvider',
  'CPUExecutionProvider']`, `quarry doctor` =
  `CPUExecutionProvider (onnx/model_int8.onnx)`.
- After manual `uv pip install --python <tool-venv-python>
  onnxruntime-gpu`: both dist-infos coexist,
  `get_available_providers()` = `['TensorrtExecutionProvider',
  'CUDAExecutionProvider', 'CPUExecutionProvider']`.  CUDA works.

## Changes

1. **Port the shell-level GPU swap block** (install.sh lines 86-129)
   into `install-server.sh`, `install-client.sh`, `install-both.sh`.
   Runs AFTER `uv tool install --force` (which re-pins the CPU wheel
   from `pyproject.toml`) and BEFORE `quarry install` (so the
   service-managed daemon starts with CUDA providers available on
   first load).
2. **Belt-and-suspenders daemon restart** in `install-server.sh` and
   `install-both.sh`: `systemctl --user restart quarry` /
   `launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u)/com.punt-labs.quarry` between
   `quarry install` and the health check.  `_systemd_install` and
   `_launchd_install` already restart the service when one is
   registered, but this closes a window where a stale daemon with old
   in-memory imports could survive an upgrade-in-place.  Both commands
   are idempotent no-ops when the service is not registered.  The
   launchctl label matches `_LABEL` in `src/quarry/service.py:30`.
3. **New shell-integration test suite** (`tests/test_install_scripts.py`,
   9 tests, ~0.4s): invokes each script against a mock `quarry` +
   mock `uv` + mock `nvidia-smi` under a restricted `PATH` (host
   `/usr/bin` excluded so a real `nvidia-smi` on the CI test host
   can't bypass the "no GPU" branch).  Mocks record invocations to a
   log file; tests assert the required call ordering
   (`uv tool install --force` -> `uv pip uninstall onnxruntime` ->
   `uv pip install onnxruntime-gpu` -> `quarry install`), plus
   negative cases (no `nvidia-smi` -> no swap,
   `install-client.sh` never calls `quarry install`).  Also runs
   `shellcheck -x` against every install script as a parameterized
   test, so Class 5 violations fail `make check` going forward.
4. **README.md install URLs** repinned to the fix commit SHA
   `9b0b2819380bc4472822c9164ea4629587a2a52f`.
5. **CHANGELOG.md** updated under `## [Unreleased]`.

## Merge strategy

**Must be merged via merge-commit (not squash or rebase).**  Same
constraint as #214: the README URLs reference the fix commit SHA
(`9b0b2819380bc4472822c9164ea4629587a2a52f`), which a rebase or squash
would rewrite, breaking the one-liner install for every user who runs
it from README on main.

## Test plan

- [x] `shellcheck -x` clean on all four install scripts (gated in CI
  via the new parameterized test).
- [x] `tests/test_install_scripts.py` — 9 passed in 0.40s.
- [x] `make check` — 1318 passed, 22 deselected (integration),
  0 lint / 0 type errors.
- [ ] End-to-end verification on okinos after merge: fresh
  `uv tool uninstall punt-quarry`, run install-server.sh one-liner
  from README, confirm `quarry doctor` reports
  `CUDAExecutionProvider` and `get_available_providers()` contains
  `CUDAExecutionProvider`.

## Follow-up beads (filed before writing the fix)

- `quarry-mxi9` (P2 bug): Root-cause the Python-side
  `ensure_gpu_runtime()` silent failure — reports rc=0 + "installed"
  but the wheel is absent afterward.  Blocker for ever dropping the
  shell-level swap.  Needs rmh.
- `quarry-0z84` (P3 task): Factor the 40-line block into a shared
  sourced fragment so this drift can't recur.  Non-trivial under the
  `curl | sh` flow — deferred to a follow-up PR.
- `quarry-yq2u` (P3 bug): `doctor._check_claude_code_mcp` hardcoded
  `timeout=10` on `claude mcp list`, slow cross-platform.
- `quarry-6ova` (P1 bug): LanceDB fragment corruption spotted in the
  okinos daemon logs during this investigation — manifest version
  `18446744073709411055` (u64::MAX - 6), fragment 28768 missing.

Primary bead: `quarry-e4c2`.

Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code).

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Touches user-facing `curl | sh` install scripts and service restart behavior, so ordering/command changes could affect real installs on macOS/Linux. Risk is mitigated by new end-to-end mocked shell tests plus a `shellcheck` gate in CI.
> 
> **Overview**
> Fixes a regression in `install-server.sh`, `install-client.sh`, and `install-both.sh` by reintroducing the shell-level NVIDIA detection and `onnxruntime`→`onnxruntime-gpu` swap (running after `uv tool install --force`, and before any `quarry install`), restoring CUDA providers on GPU hosts.
> 
> Adds a belt-and-suspenders daemon restart after `quarry install` in the server/both flows to ensure the service picks up the swapped tool venv before health checks, and introduces `tests/test_install_scripts.py` plus a CI step to install `shellcheck` so installer ordering and linting are continuously enforced. README install one-liners are repinned to the new commit SHA and the changelog is updated to document the fix.
> 
> <sup>Reviewed by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/bugbot) for commit e84b83abad8b953ad8b6ed3321cf410d8d4a88b8. Bugbot is set up for automated code reviews on this repo. Configure [here](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard/bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: Handle C-modules extended by frozen Python modules (ESP32 machine/PCNT)
items.body:
Some MicroPython ports ship frozen Python modules that *extend* a C-module rather than replace it (e.g. `ports/esp32/modules/machine.py` adds the `PCNT` pulse-counter class to the C `machine` module, first introduced in v1.26.0). Previously, `machine` was blanket-filtered from frozen stubs to prevent overwriting the better merged stub — silently discarding port-specific definitions like `PCNT`.

## Approach

Instead of skipping the frozen stub entirely, detect that it is a *complementary* extension and append only the definitions not already present in the target stub.

### `src/stubber/modcat.py`
- Added `COMPLEMENTARY_FROZEN_MODULES: Dict[str, List[str]]` — maps port → list of frozen modules that are partial extensions of their C counterpart (initial entry: `{"esp32": ["machine"]}`).
- Improved inline comment on the `machine` entry in `STUBS_COPY_FILTER[FROZEN]` to explain the new handling.

### `src/stubber/publish/stubpackage.py`
- `get_top_level_names(source: str) -> Set[str]` — returns all top-level class/function/variable names from a stub/source file using **libcst**, preserving formatting, comments and decorators.
- `append_new_definitions(target_path, complement_path) -> bool` — appends definitions from `complement_path` that are absent from `target_path` using `module.code_for_node()` to extract code fragments exactly; no duplicates, dunder names skipped.
- `copy_folder()` — when a filtered frozen stub belongs to a port listed in `COMPLEMENTARY_FROZEN_MODULES`, call `append_new_definitions()` rather than silently skipping:
  - Target exists → merge new definitions in.
  - Target absent → copy as-is (normal frozen stub behaviour).

```python
# After the change, building an esp32 package will produce a machine.pyi that
# contains the full merged machine stub *plus* the ESP32-specific PCNT class:

# --- machine.pyi (truncated) ---
class Pin: ...
class UART: ...
# ... all standard machine classes ...

# Definitions below are from the frozen complementary module
class PCNT:
    IRQ_ZERO: int
    def __init__(self, unit: int, pin: Any, *, min: int = -32768, max: int = 32767) -> None: ...
    def value(self) -> int: ...
```

### Tests & test data
- `tests/data/stub_merge/micropython-v1_26_0-frozen/esp32/GENERIC/machine.pyi` — minimal ESP32 frozen stub fixture (v1.26.0, the version when the PCNT construct first appeared) containing both a known class (`Pin`) and the new `PCNT` class.
- `tests/publish/test_complementary.py` — 27 tests covering all branches of the new code:
  - `get_top_level_names`: basic, dunders, syntax errors, empty, un-annotated assigns, compound statements, async functions, non-Name attribute targets
  - `append_new_definitions`: adds new class, no duplicates, missing files, invalid complement syntax, un-annotated/annotated assigns, compound statements, attribute-target assigns, mixed new/existing/dunder assignments, integration with real fixtures
  - `copy_folder`: esp32 merge into existing target, copy-as-is when target absent, no-new-defs (unchanged target), non-esp32 port skip, empty source dir, non-FROZEN filtered stub skip, unfiltered normal copy, directory-named-`*.pyi` skip

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> 
> ----
> 
> *This section details on the original issue you should resolve*
> 
> <issue_title>Handle C-modules that are extended by a frozen module (ESP32 - machine.py)</issue_title>
> <issue_description>Some modules are built of a combination of : 
> - C 
> - Frozen python files.
> 
> Stubber does not handle this well, not at all in fact.
> 
> The first encounter is:
> 
>  - https://github.com/micropython/micropython/blob/master/ports/esp32/modules/machine.py
>  - introduced in : https://github.com/micropython/micropython/commit/327655905e9f523070301f2f35459197d46db4fb 
> 
> This was unexpected - and breaks the stub merge/collation by overwriting machine.pyi with a partial implementation
> 
> ## breakdown
> - manifest contains partial/complementary machine.py module
> - this is saved as a frozen module that should be included 
> - it is then turned into a stub ( mypy stubgen ) 
> - that stub is then enriched with the reference stub classes and type info - which ironically made it harder to detect ( stubber enrich )
> - during the final build proces the partial `frozen.machine.pyi` overwrote the intended `machine.pyi`
> 
> 
> ## Current workaround : 
> 
> skip copying machine.pyi from the frozen stubs altogether 
> 
> STUBS_COPY_FILTER = {
>     StubSource.FROZEN: [
>         "machine",  #  esp32.frozen.machine.py is used to implement PCNT
>         # https://github.com/micropython/micropython/blob/master/ports/esp32/modules/machine.py
>     ]
> 
> ## Future 
> - is this a common pattern with more internal + frozen modules ?
> - limit the enhancement of the fozen.machine.py 
> - append the additional methods & classes 
> - add documentation for these classes 
> - add the classes to the reference ?
> - add @mp_availability(port="esp32") </issue_description>
>

>

items.title: feat: auto-spawn Python Kafka services when server.js starts
items.body:
db/kafka.js connect() now spawns producer.py (port 8001) and consumer.py (port 8002) as child processes before polling their /health endpoints. disconnect() kills both processes. process.on("exit") ensures cleanup on crash or Ctrl+C.

Single command to run everything:
  DB_ADAPTER=kafka KAFKA_BROKER=localhost:9092 node server.js

https://claude.ai/code/session_013vrZexsAXRRbyfa67RN4Nx

items.title: C Struct Visualizer: bundle, folder drop, MIME fix, IE fix
items.body:
## Summary

- **Fix MIME types**: Force `text/javascript` for `.js` files at startup — fixes the "expected a JS module script but server responded with text/plain" error that broke all functionality on some systems (especially Windows)
- **Add folder drop support**: Drop an entire project folder onto the upload zone — recursively walks the directory tree and finds all `.c`/`.h`/`.cpp`/`.hpp` files automatically using `webkitGetAsEntry()`
- **Add Browse Files / Browse Folder buttons** in the upload zone with native folder picker via `webkitdirectory`
- **Fix drag-and-drop**: Prevent browser default file-open behavior with document-level `dragover`/`drop` handlers
- **Fix IE compatibility mode**: Add `X-UA-Compatible IE=edge` meta tag
- **Change port to 5004**
- **Add bundle.txt** with all 23 project files

## Test plan

- [ ] `cd c-struct-visualizer && python app.py` starts on port 5004
- [ ] Page loads with correct CSS and no console errors about MIME types
- [ ] Click "Browse Files" — file picker opens, select `.h` file, structs render
- [ ] Click "Browse Folder" — folder picker opens, select a C project folder, all structs found
- [ ] Drag-and-drop a `.c` file onto the upload zone — parses correctly
- [ ] Drag-and-drop a project folder onto the upload zone — scans and finds C/H files
- [ ] Drag-and-drop onto the canvas area also works

https://claude.ai/code/session_01Ff5hdgmKZSw3RdXMic8jLt
